perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that at all times until termination or expiration of this Agreement it will hold in strict confidence and not disclose to any third party Confidential Information of the other, except as approved in writing by the other party to this Agreement, and will use the Confidential Information for no purpose other than evaluating or pursuing a business relationship with the other party to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it will endeavor to hold in confidence and avoid disclosing Confidential Information of the other to third parties, unless disclosure is deemed reasonably necessary, and will use the Confidential Information generally in connection with a potential business relationship with the other party to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'hold in strict confidence and not disclose' to 'endeavor to hold in confidence and avoid disclosing' weakens the obligation. The addition of 'unless disclosure is deemed reasonably necessary' introduces a subjective assessment that could be used to justify disclosures. Changing 'for no purpose other than evaluating or pursuing a business relationship' to 'generally in connection with a potential business relationship' broadens the scope and reduces accountability.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires a showing of actual or threatened misappropriation for trade secret protection. The original language provides a strong basis for claiming misappropriation. The modified language weakens this basis by making confidentiality less strict and introducing subjective interpretations ('reasonably necessary').
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially contradicting trade secret law. The original text mandates 'strict confidence and not disclose,' while the modified version uses 'endeavor to hold in confidence and avoid disclosing' and introduces a 'reasonably necessary' exception. This creates ambiguity and weakens the legal protection afforded to confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'improper means' under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' The modified language, by weakening this duty, arguably makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation under CUTSA, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall immediately notify the other upon discovery of any loss or unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Information of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall, upon becoming aware of a potential loss or possible unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Information of the other party, consider whether notification is warranted.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'immediately notify' to 'consider whether notification is warranted' introduces discretion. The original text mandates a clear action upon discovery, while the modified text allows a party to decide if notification is necessary, potentially delaying or avoiding it entirely.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Data Breach Notification Law requires businesses to notify individuals and the California Attorney General of security breaches involving personal information. The modified language, allowing discretion in notification, directly contradicts the mandatory notification requirement under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1798.29 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.8. PERSONAL DATA [1798 - 1798.78] ( Title 1.8 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) CHAPTER 1. Information Practices Act of 1977 [1798 - 1798.78] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) ARTICLE 7. Accounting of Disclosures [1798.25 - 1798.29] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) 1798.29. (a) Any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California (1) whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, or, (2) whose encrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the encryption key or security credential was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the agency that owns or licenses the encrypted information has a reasonable belief that the encryption key or security credential could
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces ambiguity regarding the notification of data breaches, while California Civil Code 1798.29(a) mandates disclosure of any breach following discovery or notification of the breach. The original text required immediate notification, aligning with the law's intent. The changed text, however, makes notification discretionary ('consider whether notification is warranted'), which weakens the legal obligation for immediate reporting and potentially violates the mandatory requirement of the California law. The law states 'any agency...shall disclose any breach', and the modified text allows for agencies to choose whether or not they should disclose.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, or upon written request of the other party, each party shall promptly destroy or return to the other all documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, or upon written request of the other party, each party shall make a reasonable effort to destroy or return the documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof, provided that such destruction or return is commercially feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall promptly destroy or return' to 'make a reasonable effort to destroy or return' weakens the obligation. Adding 'provided that such destruction or return is commercially feasible' introduces a subjective and potentially broad exception that could excuse compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under CUTSA, courts can issue injunctions to prevent the misuse of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the modified text makes it more difficult to prove a continued threat of misappropriation, potentially undermining the ability to obtain an injunction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'shall promptly destroy or return,' creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this by stating 'make a reasonable effort to destroy or return,' and introduces 'provided that such destruction or return is commercially feasible,' adding a subjective exception. According to the Law Explanation, this weakening makes it 'more difficult to prove a continued threat of misappropriation, potentially undermining the ability to obtain an injunction'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from California Code, CIV 3426.2 (a), states that 'threatened misappropriation may be enjoined' and 'affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order'. By making the return/destruction of confidential information subject to 'reasonable effort' and 'commercial feasibility,' the changed text directly undermines the possibility of obtaining an injunction or compelling affirmative acts, because it introduces ambiguity and potential excuses for non-compliance, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall make, have made, use or sell for any purpose any product or other item using, incorporating or derived from any Confidential Information to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party shall knowingly make, have made, use or sell for direct commercial gain any product or other item substantially derived from the core elements of the Confidential Information of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "knowingly" introduces a knowledge requirement, making it harder to prove a violation. Changing "using, incorporating or derived from" to "substantially derived from the core elements" narrows the scope of the restriction and introduces ambiguity about what constitutes "core elements" and "substantial derivation." Limiting the prohibition to "direct commercial gain" creates a loophole for indirect commercial benefits.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA prohibits the misappropriation of trade secrets. This modified language weakens the protection by introducing a knowledge requirement, narrowing the definition of what constitutes a violation, and creating a loophole for indirect commercial benefits, making it more challenging to establish misappropriation under CUTSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity by adding "knowingly", narrowing the scope to "substantially derived from the core elements", and limiting the prohibition to "direct commercial gain". According to the Law Explanation, this weakens the protection against misappropriation of trade secrets, making it more challenging to establish a violation under CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) codified as CIV 3426, which the Law Explanation posits is undermined by the changes. The original text provided a broader protection, whereas the modified text introduces elements that could allow for the use of confidential information without penalty.
